question: A 58-year-old man with known gout presents with 24 hours of severe first MTP pain, swelling, erythema, and inability to bear weight. Temperature is 37.4°C. He takes lisinopril and has CKD stage 3. He is not on urate-lowering therapy. Which acute flare management is most appropriate?
options: A) Start allopurinol 300 mg daily immediately to lower uric acid during the flare

B) Initiate colchicine (low-dose regimen if within 36 hours) or NSAID if renal function permits; add intra-articular or short oral corticosteroid if contraindications; continue established allopurinol if already on it but do not initiate ULT de novo during acute flare
C) Oral prednisone 60 mg daily for 6 months as sole long-term gout therapy
D) Arthrocentesis is never needed when gout history is known
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Initiate colchicine [low-dose regimen if within 36 hours] or NSAID if renal function permits; add intra-articular or short oral corticosteroid if contraindications; continue established allopurinol if already on it but do not initiate ULT de novo during acute flare).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Acute gout classically presents as excruciating monoarticular inflammation, most often the first metatarsophalangeal joint (podagra), with rapid onset over hours, marked erythema, warmth, and functional limitation. Low-grade fever may occur. In established gout, clinical diagnosis is often sufficient, but arthrocentesis remains the gold standard when infection cannot be excluded (especially with high fever, immunocompromise, or atypical joint) because septic arthritis is an orthopedic emergency.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Hyperuricemia leads to monosodium urate crystal deposition in joints and soft tissues. Acute flares result from crystal shedding triggering NLRP3 inflammasome activation and IL-1–mediated neutrophilic inflammation—not from changing serum urate alone during the attack. Thiazide-like agents and CKD reduce urate clearance, increasing flare risk.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
When the presentation is typical and systemic signs mild, treat empirically after assessing infection risk. Synovial fluid shows negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals under polarized light; WBC elevation does not distinguish gout from sepsis. Serum uric acid may be normal during acute inflammation and should not be used to rule out gout. Plain radiographs are usually normal early; ultrasound or dual-energy CT can support diagnosis in atypical cases.

MANAGEMENT:
Treat the flare first: low-dose colchicine (0.6 mg then 0.3 mg one hour later on day 1, then 0.6 mg daily) is most effective within 36 hours per ACR guidelines; avoid high-dose regimens in CKD. NSAIDs (naproxen, indomethacin) are effective if eGFR and cardiovascular/GI risk permit. Oral prednisone (30–40 mg daily tapering over 5–10 days) or intra-articular glucocorticoid are preferred when colchicine/NSAIDs contraindicated. Do not start allopurinol de novo during an acute flare (may prolong inflammation); if already on ULT, continue it. After flare resolution, initiate or uptitrate allopurinol/febuxostat with prophylaxis (colchicine or low-dose NSAID) to target urate below 6 mg/dL.

Primary care should address modifiable triggers (diuretics, alcohol, obesity) and educate on flare prophylaxis when starting ULT.

KEY POINTS:
Acute gout flare: anti-inflammatory therapy (colchicine, NSAID, or steroid)—do not initiate allopurinol de novo during the attack. Arthrocentesis when septic arthritis cannot be excluded. Long-term ULT with treat-to-target urate <6 mg/dL prevents recurrence.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Anti-inflammatory flare therapy; defer new ULT initiation).
- Classic podagra with known gout warrants prompt anti-inflammatory treatment.
- Low-dose colchicine within 36 hours, NSAID if renal/GI risk allows, or corticosteroid if contraindicated.
- Do not start allopurinol during acute flare; continue if already prescribed. Arthrocentesis when infection suspected.

question: A 74-year-old woman reports 6 weeks of bilateral shoulder and hip girdle aching stiffness, difficulty raising arms to comb hair, and morning stiffness lasting 2 hours. She has lost 4 kg unintentionally and reports new temporal headache with jaw claudication when chewing. ESR is 86 mm/hr. Which management is most appropriate?
options: A) Reassurance and ibuprofen only; recheck ESR in 6 months
B) Start prednisone 40–60 mg daily immediately and arrange urgent temporal artery biopsy; do not delay steroids for biopsy when GCA is suspected
C) Begin methotrexate monotherapy and defer steroids until biopsy confirms diagnosis
D) MRI lumbar spine to evaluate mechanical back pain before any treatment
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Start prednisone 40–60 mg daily immediately and arrange urgent temporal artery biopsy; do not delay steroids for biopsy when GCA is suspected).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) presents in adults over 50 with bilateral proximal girdle pain and stiffness (shoulders, hips, neck), prolonged morning stiffness (>45 minutes), and systemic symptoms (fatigue, weight loss). Functional limitation raising arms or rising from chairs is prominent. Giant cell arteritis (GCA) occurs in roughly 15–20% of PMR patients and may manifest as new headache, jaw/tongue claudication, scalp tenderness, or visual symptoms. Markedly elevated ESR/CRP supports the diagnosis.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
PMR and GCA share granulomatous large-vessel inflammation; GCA involves branches of the external carotid, especially temporal arteries, with risk of anterior ischemic optic neuropathy causing irreversible blindness. IL-6–driven inflammation explains elevated acute-phase reactants and steroid responsiveness.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
PMR is clinical (2012 ACR/EULAR classification criteria include age, bilateral shoulder pain, elevated CRP, morning stiffness). GCA suspicion mandates same-day evaluation: temporal artery exam (beading, tenderness, reduced pulse), consider temporal artery ultrasound (halo sign), and biopsy within 1–2 weeks of steroid start (granulomatous inflammation may persist despite treatment). When jaw claudication or visual symptoms present, treat as GCA immediately. Exclude mimics: rotator cuff disease (asymmetric, normal ESR), hypothyroidism, malignancy, rheumatoid arthritis.

MANAGEMENT:
When GCA features coexist with PMR, start high-dose prednisone (40–60 mg daily) immediately—do not delay for biopsy because vision loss is preventable but not reversible once established. Arrange temporal artery biopsy promptly. PMR without GCA typically responds to prednisone 12.5–25 mg daily with slow taper over 1–2 years; relapse common if tapered too quickly. Methotrexate or tocilizumab may be steroid-sparing adjuncts in relapsing GCA per rheumatology co-management. Monitor bone protection, glucose, and blood pressure on prolonged steroids.

Primary care must educate patients to report sudden vision loss, diplopia, or new headache urgently.

KEY POINTS:
PMR with jaw claudication and high ESR: treat as GCA—immediate high-dose prednisone and urgent biopsy without delaying steroids. PMR alone uses lower-dose prednisone with gradual taper. Ibuprofen alone or deferring treatment risks permanent vision loss.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Immediate high-dose prednisone + urgent temporal artery biopsy for suspected GCA).
- Bilateral girdle stiffness with elevated ESR plus jaw claudication suggests PMR with giant cell arteritis.
- Start prednisone 40–60 mg daily immediately; do not delay for biopsy when GCA suspected.
- Ibuprofen alone, methotrexate without steroids, or lumbar MRI delay sight-saving treatment.

question: A 42-year-old man with plaque psoriasis presents with 4 months of painful swelling of the second and third fingers (sausage digits), nail pitting, and asymmetric oligoarthritis involving DIP and PIP joints. Back pain is minimal. RF and anti-CCP are negative. Which diagnosis and initial management are most appropriate?
options: A) Rheumatoid arthritis; start methotrexate immediately without dermatology input
B) Psoriatic arthritis; refer to rheumatology and consider NSAIDs plus targeted therapy (e.g., methotrexate or biologic per severity); coordinate skin disease management
C) Osteoarthritis; recommend acetaminophen only and no further evaluation
D) Gout; start allopurinol without joint evaluation
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Psoriatic arthritis; refer to rheumatology and consider NSAIDs plus targeted therapy [e.g., methotrexate or biologic per severity]; coordinate skin disease management).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Psoriatic arthritis (PsA) occurs in up to 30% of psoriasis patients, often after skin disease but occasionally preceding rash. Key patterns include asymmetric oligoarthritis, DIP involvement (uncommon in RA), dactylitis (diffuse digit swelling), enthesitis (Achilles, plantar fascia), spondylitis, and nail changes (pitting, onycholysis). Negative RF and anti-CCP help distinguish from seropositive RA, though overlap syndromes exist.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
PsA is an immune-mediated inflammatory arthropathy linked to IL-17/IL-23 and TNF pathways, sharing genetic susceptibility (HLA-C*06:02) with psoriasis. Enthesitis reflects inflammation at tendon/ligament insertions. Untreated PsA causes erosive joint damage, functional disability, and increased cardiovascular mortality independent of psoriasis severity.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Classification uses CASPAR criteria (psoriasis, dactylitis, nail dystrophy, negative RF, juxta-articular new bone, family history). X-rays may show pencil-in-cup deformity or periarticular erosions. Evaluate for axial involvement (inflammatory back pain), uveitis symptoms, and metabolic comorbidities. Screen for latent TB and hepatitis B/C before biologics.

MANAGEMENT:
Refer to rheumatology when PsA suspected—early DMARD/biologic therapy prevents irreversible damage. NSAIDs improve symptoms but do not halt progression. Conventional DMARDs include methotrexate, sulfasalazine (peripheral disease), and leflunomide. Biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL-17/IL-23 agents) are highly effective for skin and joints. Coordinate with dermatology; treat-to-target strategies aim for minimal disease activity. Lifestyle: weight management, smoking cessation, cardiovascular risk reduction.

Primary care initiates referral, manages comorbidities, and monitors infection risk on immunosuppression.

KEY POINTS:
DIP arthritis, dactylitis, nail pitting with psoriasis = psoriatic arthritis—not RA or OA. Rheumatology referral for DMARD/biologic therapy prevents erosive disease. Do not treat as gout or simple OA without evaluation.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Psoriatic arthritis with rheumatology referral and targeted therapy).
- Sausage digits, DIP involvement, nail pitting, and psoriasis indicate psoriatic arthritis.
- Negative RF/CCP distinguishes from typical RA; early DMARD/biologic therapy prevents joint damage.
- NSAIDs alone or mislabeling as OA/gout delays disease-modifying treatment.

question: A 28-year-old man reports 8 months of insidious low back pain and buttock stiffness worse in the morning lasting over 1 hour, improving with exercise. He has no trauma history. Exam shows reduced lumbar flexion (modified Schober test abnormal) and tenderness at Achilles insertion. CRP is elevated; HLA-B27 is positive. Pelvic X-ray shows early bilateral sacroiliitis. Which initial management is most appropriate?
options: A) Strict bed rest and lumbar corset indefinitely
B) NSAIDs as first-line, physical therapy emphasizing spinal mobility and postural exercise, and rheumatology referral for assessment of biologic therapy if inadequate response
C) Immediate spinal fusion surgery
D) Oral prednisone 60 mg daily long-term without rheumatology follow-up
explanation:
CORRECT: B (NSAIDs as first-line, physical therapy emphasizing spinal mobility and postural exercise, and rheumatology referral for assessment of biologic therapy if inadequate response).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Axial spondyloarthritis (including ankylosing spondylitis when radiographic sacroiliitis present) classically affects young men with inflammatory back pain: onset before age 40, insidious course, morning stiffness >30 minutes, improvement with activity, and nocturnal pain alternating sides. Extra-articular features include enthesitis (Achilles, plantar fascia), uveitis (acute unilateral red eye), psoriasis, and inflammatory bowel disease.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Spinal and sacroiliac inflammation driven by IL-17/IL-23 and TNF pathways leads to syndesmophyte formation and reduced spinal mobility if untreated. HLA-B27 is associated (~90% in AS) but not diagnostic alone—present in 6–8% of general population. Elevated CRP correlates with active inflammation.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
ASAS criteria incorporate inflammatory back pain features plus MRI sacroiliitis (bone marrow edema) or radiographic sacroiliitis. MRI is more sensitive early when X-rays normal. Distinguish from mechanical LBP (older age, no morning stiffness, no improvement with exercise). Screen for IBD symptoms and acute anterior uveitis.

MANAGEMENT:
First-line: continuous or on-demand NSAIDs (naproxen, indomethacin) improve pain and may slow radiographic progression if used consistently. Physical therapy maintains posture, chest expansion, and flexibility; discourage prolonged immobilization. Smokers have worse outcomes—counsel cessation. Rheumatology referral for persistent activity despite NSAIDs: TNF inhibitors, IL-17 inhibitors, or JAK inhibitors reduce inflammation and functional impairment. Long-term high-dose prednisone is not standard for axial disease.

Primary care monitors for uveitis symptoms (urgent ophthalmology) and cardiovascular risk.

KEY POINTS:
Inflammatory back pain in young adult with sacroiliitis: NSAIDs + PT + rheumatology—not bed rest or early surgery. HLA-B27 supports but does not alone diagnose spondyloarthritis. Biologics when NSAIDs insufficient.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (NSAIDs, PT, rheumatology referral for possible biologics).
- Morning stiffness >1 hour, improvement with exercise, young age, sacroiliitis = axial spondyloarthritis.
- First-line NSAIDs and physical therapy; rheumatology for biologic therapy if inadequate response.
- Bed rest, immediate fusion, or chronic high-dose prednisone are not appropriate initial management.

question: A 45-year-old woman presents with 2 years of widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, unrefreshing sleep, and cognitive fog. Exam shows tenderness in multiple soft-tissue sites without joint swelling or synovitis. ESR, CRP, TSH, and CMP are normal. Which management plan is most appropriate?
options: A) Order extensive autoimmune serologies and MRI whole body before any treatment
B) Confirm fibromyalgia clinically, educate on diagnosis, prioritize graded aerobic exercise and CBT, consider duloxetine or pregabalin for persistent symptoms; avoid chronic opioids
C) Prescribe long-acting oxycodone as first-line therapy
D) Diagnose seronegative rheumatoid arthritis and start methotrexate empirically
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Confirm fibromyalgia clinically, educate on diagnosis, prioritize graded aerobic exercise and CBT, consider duloxetine or pregabalin for persistent symptoms; avoid chronic opioids).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Fibromyalgia presents with chronic widespread pain (>3 months), fatigue, non-restorative sleep, cognitive dysfunction (“fibro fog”), and often comorbid mood disorders, irritable bowel syndrome, and migraine. Tender points or widespread pain index with symptom severity (2016 ACR criteria) support diagnosis. Normal inflammatory markers and absence of synovitis distinguish from inflammatory arthritis.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Central sensitization and altered pain processing in the CNS (augmented nociceptive signaling, reduced descending inhibition) explain disproportionate pain without tissue-destructive inflammation. Sleep disruption and psychological stress amplify symptoms. Fibromyalgia is not a diagnosis of exclusion for inflammatory disease when criteria met, but targeted workup excludes mimics (hypothyroidism, polymyalgia, RA, sleep apnea).

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Clinical diagnosis after history, exam, and basic labs (CBC, CMP, TSH, ESR/CRP). Avoid excessive testing that reinforces illness anxiety. Screen for depression, anxiety, PTSD, and obstructive sleep apnea. Differentiate from inflammatory myopathies (weakness, elevated CK) and statin myopathy.

MANAGEMENT:
Patient education that fibromyalgia is real, treatable, and non-degenerative improves adherence. First-line nonpharmacologic: graded aerobic exercise (walking, swimming, tai chi)—most evidence-based intervention. CBT and sleep hygiene address catastrophizing and non-restorative sleep. Pharmacotherapy adjuncts: duloxetine or milnacipran (SNRIs), pregabalin or gabapentin. Tramadol may be considered short-term; chronic opioids worsen outcomes and hyperalgesia. Co-manage mood disorders and comorbid pain conditions.

Primary care provides continuity, exercise prescription, and avoids polypharmacy.

KEY POINTS:
Fibromyalgia: exercise + CBT first; SNRI/anticonvulsant if needed—avoid chronic opioids. Normal ESR/CRP without synovitis; do not empirically treat as RA. Extensive imaging/serology before therapy delays effective care.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Education, graded exercise, CBT; duloxetine/pregabalin if needed; avoid opioids).
- Widespread pain, fatigue, sleep disturbance, normal inflammatory markers fit fibromyalgia.
- First-line: aerobic exercise and CBT; consider duloxetine or pregabalin for persistent symptoms.
- Chronic opioids, empiric methotrexate, or exhaustive testing before treatment are inappropriate.

question: A 52-year-old asymptomatic man with no family history asks about colorectal cancer screening. He has no prior polyps, inflammatory bowel disease, or genetic syndrome. Which screening approach aligns with current USPSTF recommendations?
options: A) Begin screening at age 50 with colonoscopy only; age 45 is not recommended
B) Offer screening starting at age 45 using colonoscopy every 10 years, annual FIT/FOBT, or other approved strategies per shared decision-making
C) No screening needed until symptomatic rectal bleeding occurs
D) Abdominal CT colonography every year starting at age 30
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Offer screening starting at age 45 using colonoscopy every 10 years, annual FIT/FOBT, or other approved strategies per shared decision-making).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Colorectal cancer (CRC) is often asymptomatic early; when symptomatic may present with occult bleeding, anemia, change in stool caliber, or weight loss—by which stage may be advanced. Average-risk adults benefit from screening before symptoms because removal of adenomatous polyps prevents progression to invasive carcinoma.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Most CRC arises via the adenoma–carcinoma sequence driven by cumulative somatic mutations (APC, KRAS, p53). Age and modifiable factors (red/processed meat, obesity, smoking, alcohol) increase risk. Lynch syndrome and familial adenomatous polyposis require earlier and more intensive surveillance—not average-risk protocols.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
USPSTF 2021 recommends CRC screening for adults 45–75 at average risk (Grade B). Options include colonoscopy every 10 years, annual high-sensitivity fecal immunochemical test (FIT) or guaiac FOBT, FIT-DNA every 1–3 years (Cologuard), flexible sigmoidoscopy every 5 years (± FIT), or CT colonography every 5 years. Shared decision-making weighs invasiveness, interval, prep burden, and follow-up colonoscopy for positive non-colonoscopic tests. Screening may individualize for ages 76–85 based on health and prior screening; generally not recommended after 85.

MANAGEMENT:
Positive FIT mandates diagnostic colonoscopy—not repeat FIT alone. Colonoscopy with polypectomy reduces CRC incidence and mortality. Primary care orders appropriate test, ensures completion, and tracks abnormal results. High-risk patients (first-degree relative with CRC <60, or two relatives, IBD, prior adenomas) need earlier colonoscopy—often age 40 or 10 years before youngest affected relative.

Counsel on diet, physical activity, and smoking cessation as adjunct prevention.

KEY POINTS:
Average-risk CRC screening starts at age 45 per USPSTF—not wait for symptoms or age 50 only. Multiple approved modalities; positive FIT requires colonoscopy. High-risk patients need earlier colonoscopy, not average-risk FIT alone.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Screen from age 45 with colonoscopy, FIT, or other approved tests).
- USPSTF recommends colorectal cancer screening for average-risk adults starting at age 45.
- Options include colonoscopy every 10 years, annual FIT, sigmoidoscopy, or CT colonography per shared decision-making.
- Waiting for bleeding or screening only at 30 with annual CT is not guideline-concordant.

question: A 55-year-old woman has an incidental 1.2-cm solid thyroid nodule discovered on CT for unrelated reasons. She is euthyroid without compressive symptoms. Neck ultrasound shows a hypoechoic nodule with irregular margins and microcalcifications. TSH is normal. Which next step is most appropriate?
options: A) Empiric levothyroxine suppression therapy to shrink the nodule
B) Fine-needle aspiration biopsy because sonographic features are suspicious regardless of size >1 cm
C) Observation only; microcalcifications are benign in all nodules
D) Total thyroidectomy without tissue diagnosis
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Fine-needle aspiration biopsy because sonographic features are suspicious regardless of size >1 cm).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Thyroid nodules are common (>50% of adults on ultrasound), but only 5–15% are malignant. Incidental nodules on cross-sectional imaging require dedicated neck ultrasound. Most cancers are papillary thyroid carcinoma with excellent prognosis when treated appropriately. Red flags include rapid growth, hoarseness, dysphagia, fixed hard mass, and cervical lymphadenopathy.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Thyroid malignancy risk correlates with ultrasound features: hypoechogenicity, irregular margins, taller-than-wide shape, microcalcifications (psammoma bodies), and extrathyroidal extension. TSH suppression does not reliably reduce malignancy risk and may cause subclinical hyperthyroidism harm.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
ATA guidelines stratify nodules by sonographic pattern. Suspicious features (highly suspicious or suspicious categories) warrant FNA at ≥1 cm; same features at 0.5–1 cm may also merit biopsy depending on pattern and patient factors. Very suspicious nodules ≥1 cm with microcalcifications, irregular margins require FNA. Check TSH; if suppressed, radionuclide scan identifies autonomously functioning nodule (lower malignancy risk, usually no FNA). FNA results use Bethesda classification guiding surgery vs observation.

MANAGEMENT:
Benign FNA: serial ultrasound surveillance. Malignant or indeterminate categories: endocrine surgery referral. Do not perform thyroidectomy without cytopathology except rare cases with gross invasion. Primary care coordinates ultrasound, FNA, and referral; avoids empiric levothyroxine suppression (not recommended for benign nodules per ATA).

KEY POINTS:
Suspicious thyroid nodule (hypoechoic, irregular margins, microcalcifications) ≥1 cm needs FNA—not suppression or blind surgery. TSH first; radionuclide scan if suppressed. Bethesda FNA results guide next steps.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (FNA for suspicious 1.2-cm nodule with worrisome ultrasound features).
- Hypoechoic nodule with irregular margins and microcalcifications is suspicious for malignancy.
- Fine-needle aspiration is indicated for nodules ≥1 cm with these features.
- Levothyroxine suppression, observation ignoring features, or surgery without diagnosis are incorrect.

question: A 68-year-old man presents with 3 months of fatigue, back pain, and recurrent pneumonia. Labs show hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL, calcium 11.4 mg/dL, creatinine 1.8 mg/dL, and total protein 9.8 g/dL. SPEP reveals an IgG monoclonal spike. X-ray shows multiple lytic skull lesions. Which diagnosis and initial primary care action are most appropriate?
options: A) Iron deficiency anemia; start oral iron and repeat CBC in 6 months
B) Multiple myeloma; urgent hematology/oncology referral for confirmatory workup (bone marrow, imaging) and treatment planning—avoid delaying for oral iron alone
C) Benign monoclonal gammopathy; no follow-up needed
D) Metastatic prostate cancer is the only explanation for lytic bone lesions
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Multiple myeloma; urgent hematology/oncology referral for confirmatory workup [bone marrow, imaging] and treatment planning—avoid delaying for oral iron alone).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Multiple myeloma (MM) presents in older adults with anemia, bone pain (especially back), pathologic fractures, hypercalcemia, renal insufficiency, and recurrent infections from impaired humoral immunity. CRAB criteria define active disease: hyperCalcemia, Renal failure, Anemia, Bone lesions. Lytic “punched-out” lesions on plain film and monoclonal protein on SPEP/UPEP are classic.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Clonal plasma cell proliferation in marrow produces monoclonal immunoglobulin (M-protein), suppresses normal immunoglobulins, activates osteoclasts (RANKL pathway) causing lytic bone destruction, and may cast nephropathy (light chains) leading to renal failure. Hypercalcemia results from bone resorption.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Initial labs: CBC, CMP (calcium, creatinine), SPEP with immunofixation, serum free light chains, UPEP. Suspect MM when M-protein plus CRAB features. Quantify M-protein and beta-2 microglobulin. Skeletal survey (low-dose whole-body CT or PET-CT preferred over plain survey at referral centers) identifies lytic lesions. Bone marrow biopsy confirms clonal plasma cells ≥10%. Differentiate monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS: M-protein <3 g/dL, no CRAB, <10% plasma cells) which needs surveillance only.

MANAGEMENT:
Urgent hematology/oncology referral for induction therapy (proteasome inhibitors, immunomodulatory drugs, steroids, anti-CD38 antibodies) and autologous transplant eligibility assessment. Treat hypercalcemia (IV fluids, bisphosphonates), avoid nephrotoxins, consider infection prophylaxis. Primary care supports referrals, monitors complications, and does not attribute CRAB syndrome to iron deficiency.

KEY POINTS:
Anemia + hypercalcemia + lytic lesions + M-spike = multiple myeloma until proven otherwise—urgent heme/onc referral. MGUS lacks CRAB features. Oral iron alone delays life-saving treatment.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Multiple myeloma with urgent hematology/oncology referral).
- CRAB features (hyperCalcemia, Renal failure, Anemia, Bone lesions) with M-spike indicate active myeloma.
- Confirm with marrow biopsy and advanced imaging; initiate specialist-directed systemic therapy.
- Iron deficiency alone, ignoring MGUS follow-up, or attributing solely to prostate cancer misses the diagnosis.

question: A 70-year-old fair-skinned farmer has a 7-mm pigmented lesion on the upper back with asymmetry, irregular borders, color variation, and diameter enlargement over 6 months. No palpable adenopathy. Which primary care action is most appropriate?
options: A) Prescribe topical antifungal cream and reassess in 1 year
B) Full-thickness excisional biopsy (or referral for biopsy) with narrow margins for histopathologic diagnosis—do not shave biopsy pigmented lesions when melanoma suspected
C) Cryotherapy without histologic examination
D) Reassure that all pigmented lesions in elderly are benign seborrheic keratoses
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Full-thickness excisional biopsy [or referral for biopsy] with narrow margins for histopathologic diagnosis—do not shave biopsy pigmented lesions when melanoma suspected).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Melanoma may arise de novo or from dysplastic nevi. ABCDE criteria guide suspicion: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter >6 mm (though smaller lesions can be melanoma), Evolution (change over time). The “ugly duckling” sign (one lesion unlike others) adds sensitivity. Advanced disease may show satellite lesions or regional lymphadenopathy.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
UV exposure drives cumulative DNA damage in melanocytes; intermittent intense sunburns and fair skin (Fitzpatrick I–II) increase risk. Superficial spreading, nodular, lentigo maligna, and acral lentiginous subtypes vary by growth pattern. Breslow thickness and ulceration determine staging and prognosis; early detection is critical because thin melanomas have excellent survival.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Any suspicious pigmented lesion warrants biopsy—not observation or destructive treatment without pathology. Preferred: full-thickness excisional biopsy with 1–3 mm clinical margins encompassing entire lesion if feasible. Incisional biopsy acceptable on cosmetically sensitive areas when excision impractical—sample thickest portion. Avoid shave biopsy when melanoma suspected because it understages Breslow depth. Dermoscopy by trained clinicians improves specificity; refer if uncertain.

MANAGEMENT:
After diagnosis, wide local excision per Breslow thickness, sentinel lymph node biopsy when indicated (per AJCC staging), and oncology referral for advanced disease. Primary care performs skin cancer screening in high-risk patients, documents lesions, and ensures timely biopsy. Educate on self-examination and sun protection.

KEY POINTS:
Suspicious pigmented lesion (ABCDE): excisional biopsy for diagnosis—not cryotherapy, antifungals, or reassurance. Shave biopsy risks understaging melanoma. Early biopsy saves lives.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Excisional biopsy for histologic diagnosis when melanoma suspected).
- Asymmetric, irregular, multicolor enlarging pigmented lesion meets melanoma warning signs.
- Full-thickness excisional biopsy preferred; avoid shave biopsy that understages depth.
- Topical antifungals, cryotherapy without pathology, or benign reassurance are unsafe.

question: A 58-year-old breast cancer survivor 2 years post-chemotherapy reports persistent severe fatigue limiting daily activities. She sleeps 9 hours nightly but feels unrefreshed. Hemoglobin, TSH, and CMP are normal. Depression screen is negative. Which management is most appropriate?
options: A) Reassure that fatigue always resolves by 6 months and no intervention needed

B) Structured exercise program, sleep hygiene optimization, psychosocial support, and evaluate treatable contributors (pain, mood, medication effects); consider referral to cancer survivorship resources
C) Start long-term benzodiazepines for sleep
D) Repeat full chemotherapy for fatigue
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Structured exercise program, sleep hygiene optimization, psychosocial support, and evaluate treatable contributors [pain, mood, medication effects]; consider referral to cancer survivorship resources).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Cancer-related fatigue (CRF) is a distressing, persistent sense of exhaustion disproportionate to activity, not relieved by rest, common during and after treatment. It impairs quality of life and differs from ordinary tiredness. Survivors may report cognitive fog, reduced exercise tolerance, and functional decline years after therapy.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Multifactorial: inflammatory cytokines, HPA axis dysregulation, autonomic imbalance, sleep disruption, deconditioning, anemia (ruled out here), hypothyroidism, pain, depression/anxiety, and medication effects (tamoxifen, opioids). CRF is not a marker of occult recurrence when workup otherwise normal—but new progressive symptoms warrant oncology reassessment.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
NCCN survivorship guidelines recommend evaluating fatigue with history (onset, pattern, sleep, mood, pain, activity), exam, and targeted labs (CBC, CMP, TSH; B12 if indicated). Screen depression/anxiety with validated tools. Review medications and comorbidities (cardiac, pulmonary, sleep apnea). Distinguish from hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency (if on steroids), and cardiomyopathy when anthracyclines received.

MANAGEMENT:
First-line evidence-based interventions: physical activity (graded aerobic and resistance training—strongest evidence), CBT/behavioral activation, and energy conservation strategies. Treat contributing conditions (pain, insomnia, mood disorders). Pharmacologic stimulants (methylphenidate, modafinil) reserved for selected refractory cases after nonpharmacologic trials—not first-line. Avoid benzodiazepines long-term (worsen fatigue, fall risk). Coordinate with oncology survivorship clinics, social work, and PT.

Primary care provides longitudinal support during transition from oncology.

KEY POINTS:
Post-treatment cancer fatigue: exercise and CBT first; evaluate reversible contributors—not benzodiazepines or repeat chemotherapy. Fatigue can persist beyond 6 months; structured intervention improves function.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Exercise, sleep hygiene, psychosocial support, treat contributors, survivorship referral).
- Persistent cancer-related fatigue with normal labs needs multimodal management, not dismissal.
- Graded exercise and behavioral interventions have the strongest evidence.
- Benzodiazepines worsen function; repeat chemotherapy is not indicated for fatigue alone.

question: A 62-year-old man with hypertension and diabetes develops sudden severe substernal chest pressure radiating to the left arm, diaphoresis, and nausea 30 minutes ago. He is alert; BP 148/92, HR 98, SpO2 96% on room air. EMS ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. Which immediate action is most appropriate while arranging emergent transfer?
options: A) Administer chewable aspirin 325 mg if no contraindication, ensure supplemental oxygen only if hypoxemic, and activate emergent reperfusion pathway (PCI-capable center or fibrinolysis per protocol)—do not delay transfer for office stress test
B) Schedule outpatient exercise stress test in 1 week
C) Give sublingual nitroglycerin only and send home if pain improves
D) Withhold aspirin because he may need surgery later
explanation:
CORRECT: A (Administer chewable aspirin 325 mg if no contraindication, ensure supplemental oxygen only if hypoxemic, and activate emergent reperfusion pathway [PCI-capable center or fibrinolysis per protocol]—do not delay transfer for office stress test).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) presents with acute ischemic chest discomfort often radiating to arm, jaw, or back, with autonomic symptoms (diaphoresis, nausea). Inferior STEMI (II, III, aVF) suggests right coronary artery occlusion; assess for right ventricular involvement (hypotension, clear lungs, ST elevation V1R). Time from symptom onset to reperfusion strongly determines mortality and salvage of myocardium (“time is muscle”).

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Acute coronary thrombosis on ruptured atherosclerotic plaque causes transmural ischemia manifesting as ST elevation. Without prompt reperfusion, irreversible necrosis progresses over hours. Aspirin irreversibly inhibits platelet COX-1, reducing thrombus propagation and mortality in acute MI.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
ECG within 10 minutes of presentation; STEMI defined by ST elevation ≥1 mm in contiguous leads (or new LBBB with clinical syndrome). Serial troponins confirm myocardial injury but must not delay reperfusion when ECG diagnostic. Distinguish from pericarditis, early repolarization, and STEMI equivalents (posterior MI: ST depression V1–V3 with tall R waves).

MANAGEMENT:
Primary care/urgent setting role: call 911/EMS, aspirin 162–325 mg chewed (unless active bleeding or true aspirin allergy), nitrates if BP adequate and not RV infarct, morphine only if pain severe (use cautiously). Supplemental oxygen if SpO2 <90%; routine oxygen not beneficial if normoxic. Immediate transfer to PCI-capable center; door-to-balloon goal ≤90 minutes. If PCI unavailable within 120 minutes, fibrinolysis within 30 minutes of arrival per protocol. Beta-blockers and ACE inhibitors initiated after stabilization unless contraindicated.

Never discharge suspected STEMI or defer to outpatient stress testing.

KEY POINTS:
STEMI: aspirin + emergent reperfusion (PCI preferred)—minutes matter. Do not withhold aspirin; do not send home after nitroglycerin alone. Outpatient stress testing is contraindicated in acute STEMI.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: A (Aspirin, oxygen if hypoxemic, emergent reperfusion activation).
- ST elevation in II, III, aVF with typical symptoms indicates inferior STEMI requiring immediate reperfusion.
- Chewable aspirin 325 mg unless contraindicated; transfer to PCI-capable center without delay.
- Outpatient stress test, discharge after nitrates alone, or withholding aspirin are dangerous errors.

question: A 34-year-old woman develops acute urticaria, facial angioedema, wheezing, and hypotension within 10 minutes of eating peanuts at a restaurant. She has an epinephrine auto-injector. Which immediate management is most appropriate?
options: A) Oral diphenhydramine alone and observe in waiting room for 30 minutes
B) Intramuscular epinephrine 0.3–0.5 mg in anterolateral thigh immediately, call emergency services, place supine with legs elevated, prepare for repeat epinephrine if no improvement in 5–15 minutes
C) IV bolus epinephrine as first-line in all mild allergic reactions in the office
D) Delay epinephrine until anaphylaxis can be confirmed by tryptase level
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Intramuscular epinephrine 0.3–0.5 mg in anterolateral thigh immediately, call emergency services, place supine with legs elevated, prepare for repeat epinephrine if no improvement in 5–15 minutes).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Anaphylaxis is a rapid-onset systemic allergic reaction with skin/mucosal involvement (urticaria, angioedema) plus respiratory compromise (wheezing, stridor) and/or cardiovascular collapse (hypotension, syncope). Food (peanut, tree nuts), drugs, venom, and latex are common triggers. Biphasic reactions can occur hours later—observation after treatment is required.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation releases histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes, causing vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, bronchospasm, and laryngeal edema. Without epinephrine, progression to airway obstruction and shock can be fatal within minutes.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Clinical diagnosis when two organ systems involved after exposure. Do not delay treatment for labs. Tryptase may support diagnosis retrospectively. Distinguish from vasovagal syncope (bradycardia, pallor, rapid recovery without urticaria/wheeze) and panic attack.

MANAGEMENT:
IM epinephrine (0.01 mg/kg of 1:1000, max 0.5 mg adult) in anterolateral thigh is first-line—superior to subcutaneous route. Call EMS; position supine with legs elevated (unless vomiting/respiratory distress). Repeat epinephrine every 5–15 minutes if persistent symptoms. Adjuncts after epinephrine: albuterol for bronchospasm, IV fluids for hypotension, H1/H2 blockers and corticosteroids (do not replace epinephrine). Observe 4–6 hours minimum for food-induced anaphylaxis due to biphasic risk. Prescribe two auto-injectors, allergist referral, and action plan.

IV epinephrine reserved for refractory shock under monitoring—not first-line for uncomplicated anaphylaxis due to arrhythmia risk.

KEY POINTS:
Anaphylaxis: IM epinephrine first—antihistamines alone are insufficient. Do not wait for labs. Repeat epinephrine and EMS activation; observe for biphasic reaction.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Immediate IM epinephrine, EMS, supine positioning, repeat dose if needed).
- Urticaria, angioedema, wheezing, and hypotension after peanut exposure is anaphylaxis.
- Intramuscular epinephrine in the thigh is first-line; antihistamines alone or delayed treatment are unsafe.
- IV epinephrine is not routine first-line; tryptase should not delay epinephrine.

question: A 22-year-old man falls off a skateboard and hits his head without loss of consciousness. He has mild headache and nausea but no vomiting, amnesia, seizure, or anticoagulant use. GCS 15; exam normal including no focal neurologic deficits. Which management aligns with concussion guidelines for primary care/urgent settings?
options: A) Strict complete cognitive and physical rest until all symptoms resolve for 2 weeks, then return to full contact sport immediately

B) Brief relative rest 24–48 hours, gradual return to activity as tolerated using stepwise symptom-limited protocol; head injury instructions and return precautions; CT head not indicated for this low-risk presentation
C) Routine CT head for all minor head bumps regardless of features
D) Return to full-contact football the same day if athlete feels motivated
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Brief relative rest 24–48 hours, gradual return to activity as tolerated using stepwise symptom-limited protocol; head injury instructions and return precautions; CT head not indicated for this low-risk presentation).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Concussion (mild traumatic brain injury) causes transient neurologic dysfunction after head impact, often without LOC. Symptoms include headache, dizziness, nausea, cognitive slowing, sleep disturbance, and irritability—typically resolving over days to weeks. Red flags for intracranial injury: prolonged LOC, amnesia, worsening headache, repeated vomiting, seizure, anticoagulation, coagulopathy, intoxication, dangerous mechanism, or focal neurologic signs.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Concussive forces cause neurometabolic cascade (ionic shifts, reduced cerebral blood flow, diffuse axonal strain) rather than macroscopic hemorrhage in most cases. Second impact before recovery risks rare catastrophic edema—return-to-play protocols protect athletes.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Clinical diagnosis; CT head not required for low-risk adult concussion per CDC/ACEP criteria when GCS 15, no high-risk features, and normal exam. PECARN and Canadian CT Head Rules guide imaging in pediatric and adult populations respectively. Use validated concussion assessment tools in sports settings; symptom scales track recovery.

MANAGEMENT:
Current consensus (Berlin/Copenhagen): avoid prolonged strict rest; 24–48 hours relative rest then gradual return to light activity as symptoms permit. Stepwise return-to-sport (6 stages) requires symptom-free progression and medical clearance before contact. Educate on sleep hygiene, hydration, limited screen time initially. Warn to seek care for worsening headache, repeated vomiting, confusion, weakness, or slurred speech. School/work accommodations as needed. Most recover within 2–4 weeks; persistent symptoms warrant specialty referral.

KEY POINTS:
Low-risk concussion: brief rest then gradual return—not same-day contact sports or prolonged total rest. CT not indicated without red flags. Stepwise return-to-play prevents second-impact complications.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Brief rest, stepwise return, red-flag education; no CT for low-risk case).
- GCS 15 concussion without red flags does not require routine CT.
- 24–48 hours relative rest then symptom-limited gradual return to activity.
- Same-day full contact sport, prolonged strict rest only, or CT for all minor bumps are not guideline-concordant.

question: A 19-year-old woman ingested approximately 20 grams of acetaminophen 6 hours ago in a suicide attempt. She is asymptomatic with normal vital signs. AST/ALT and INR are normal; acetaminophen level is 180 mcg/mL. Which management is most appropriate?
options: A) Discharge home because she is asymptomatic with normal liver enzymes
B) Administer N-acetylcysteine (NAC) immediately because level is above treatment line on Rumack-Matthew nomogram—do not wait for transaminase elevation
C) Induce emesis with syrup of ipecac and observe
D) Start activated charcoal now as sole therapy regardless of timing
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Administer N-acetylcysteine [NAC] immediately because level is above treatment line on Rumack-Matthew nomogram—do not wait for transaminase elevation).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Acetaminophen toxicity has four phases: (1) 0–24 h—anorexia, nausea, possibly asymptomatic; (2) 24–72 h—RUQ pain, rising AST/ALT; (3) 72–96 h—peak hepatotoxicity, coagulopathy, encephalopathy; (4) recovery or fulminant failure. Early asymptomatic presentation does not exclude severe impending liver injury.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
At toxic doses, hepatic glucuronidation/sulfation saturate; CYP2E1 produces reactive NAPQI metabolite depleting glutathione, causing centrilobular hepatocyte necrosis. NAC replenishes glutathione and is hepatoprotective when given within 8–10 hours ideally, but benefits extend later in selected cases.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Obtain acetaminophen level at ≥4 hours post-ingestion; plot on Rumack-Matthew nomogram (treatment line 150 mcg/mL at 4 h, lower at later times). Unknown time or extended-release formulations warrant treatment at lower thresholds. Check AST/ALT, INR, creatinine, phosphate (prognostic in severe cases). Hepatotoxicity lags behind toxic levels—normal enzymes do not exclude need for NAC.

MANAGEMENT:
Level 180 at 6 hours is above treatment line—start IV or oral NAC per protocol immediately. Activated charcoal within 1–2 hours if alert and airway protected; limited benefit at 6 hours as sole therapy. Psychiatry consultation and observation for self-harm after medical stabilization. Transfer to facility capable of managing acute liver failure if deterioration. Never discharge based on absence of symptoms alone.

KEY POINTS:
Acetaminophen overdose: treat with NAC when level above nomogram line—do not wait for elevated LFTs. Asymptomatic patients can develop fulminant hepatic failure. Charcoal at 6 hours alone or ipecac is inadequate.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (NAC immediately for toxic acetaminophen level on nomogram).
- Level 180 mcg/mL at 6 hours exceeds Rumack-Matthew treatment threshold.
- N-acetylcysteine prevents hepatotoxicity; normal AST/ALT does not exclude treatment need.
- Discharge when asymptomatic, ipecac, or charcoal alone at 6 hours are inappropriate.

question: A 78-year-old nursing home resident with dementia presents with acute confusion, fever 38.9°C, heart rate 112, respiratory rate 24, and blood pressure 98/60. Urinalysis shows pyuria and nitrites. She has no cough or focal lung findings. Lactate is 2.8 mmol/L. Which management is most appropriate in the first hour?
options: A) Oral ciprofloxacin outpatient and recheck in 1 week

B) Recognize sepsis, obtain blood cultures, give IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, IV fluid resuscitation, and arrange higher level of care monitoring—treat likely urosepsis without delaying antibiotics for imaging
C) Withhold antibiotics until urine culture sensitivities return in 48 hours
D) Attribute confusion solely to dementia; no treatment needed
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Recognize sepsis, obtain blood cultures, give IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, IV fluid resuscitation, and arrange higher level of care monitoring—treat likely urosepsis without delaying antibiotics for imaging).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Sepsis is life-threatening organ dysfunction from dysregulated host response to infection. In older adults, delirium may be the primary presentation of UTI/pneumonia. Fever, tachycardia, tachypnea, hypotension, and elevated lactate suggest sepsis. Pyuria with nitrites supports urinary source but bacteriuria alone does not define infection in catheterized patients—here systemic signs indicate urosepsis.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Pathogen and host inflammatory response trigger vasodilation, capillary leak, and microvascular thrombosis causing tissue hypoperfusion and organ dysfunction. Each hour delay in appropriate antibiotics increases mortality in septic shock. Lactate >2 mmol/L reflects hypoperfusion and warrants aggressive resuscitation.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Sepsis-3: infection suspected plus SOFA increase ≥2 (often impractical in ED/urgent care); qSOFA (altered mentation, RR ≥22, SBP ≤100) aids bedside recognition. Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics when feasible without delay, urinalysis/urine culture, CBC, CMP, lactate. Chest X-ray if respiratory symptoms; avoid delaying antibiotics for CT in unstable patients.

MANAGEMENT:
Surviving Sepsis Campaign: IV crystalloid 30 mL/kg for hypotension or lactate ≥4 (adjust in heart failure); broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour (e.g., ceftriaxone ± ampicillin for UTI source per local resistance patterns). Transfer to ED/hospital for monitoring, vasopressors if refractory hypotension, source control if obstructive uropathy suspected. Oral outpatient therapy inappropriate for septic shock physiology.

Primary care in nursing facilities must have low threshold for EMS transfer.

KEY POINTS:
Sepsis with delirium, fever, hypotension, elevated lactate: IV antibiotics within 1 hour plus fluids—not outpatient oral meds or waiting for cultures. Do not dismiss as baseline dementia.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Sepsis bundle: cultures, IV antibiotics within 1 hour, fluids, escalation).
- Acute delirium with fever, tachycardia, hypotension, pyuria, and elevated lactate indicates urosepsis.
- Do not delay antibiotics for culture results or imaging when sepsis present.
- Outpatient oral antibiotics or attributing solely to dementia risks mortality.

question: A 54-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.9%) has no visual symptoms. He last had an eye exam 4 years ago. Fundoscopic exam in clinic is limited. Which screening approach is most appropriate?
options: A) No eye screening needed until the patient reports blurred vision
B) Refer for dilated comprehensive eye exam now and at least annually thereafter because diabetic retinopathy can be asymptomatic and vision-threatening
C) Visual acuity check in primary care alone is sufficient indefinitely
D) Screening only every 5 years if HbA1c remains below 10%
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Refer for dilated comprehensive eye exam now and at least annually thereafter because diabetic retinopathy can be asymptomatic and vision-threatening).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Diabetic retinopathy (DR) progresses silently until advanced—microaneurysms, hemorrhages, macular edema, and neovascularization cause irreversible vision loss if untreated. Patients may retain normal acuity until macular involvement or vitreous hemorrhage. Duration of diabetes, poor glycemic control, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and renal disease increase risk.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Chronic hyperglycemia damages retinal capillaries via advanced glycation end-products, oxidative stress, and inflammation—leading to microaneurysms, capillary non-perfusion, and VEGF-driven neovascularization in proliferative DR. Macular edema is the leading cause of vision loss in nonproliferative DR.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
ADA standards: initial dilated retinal exam at diabetes diagnosis (type 2) or within 5 years of type 1 onset, then at least annually; every 2 years if one or more normal exams and well-controlled. Nonmydriatic retinal photography in validated programs may substitute if interpreted by trained readers with timely referral for unreadable or abnormal images. Primary care fundoscopy has limited sensitivity—formal ophthalmic exam preferred.

MANAGEMENT:
Optimize glycemic control (DCCT/UKPDS: tight control reduces DR progression), blood pressure (ACEi/ARB), and lipid management. Refer abnormal screens urgently: macular edema and proliferative DR need intravitreal anti-VEGF, laser photocoagulation, or surgery. Pregnancy in diabetes requires exam each trimester. Document screening completion—gap in care here (4 years) warrants immediate referral.

KEY POINTS:
Diabetic retinopathy screening: dilated eye exam at least annually—not wait for symptoms or acuity alone. Poorly controlled type 2 DM with overdue exam needs urgent referral. Vision symptoms indicate possible advanced disease.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Dilated comprehensive eye exam now and at least annually).
- Diabetic retinopathy is often asymptomatic until advanced; annual dilated exam is standard of care.
- Type 2 diabetes requires screening at diagnosis and regularly thereafter regardless of symptoms.
- Visual acuity alone or 5-year intervals with high HbA1c miss treatable retinopathy.

question: A 65-year-old woman reports sudden painless monocular vision loss lasting 90 seconds while reading, with full recovery. She has atrial fibrillation on apixaban and carotid bruit on exam. Which evaluation is most appropriate?
options: A) Reassure because vision returned; no workup needed
B) Treat as amaurosis fugax/transient monocular vision loss—urgent evaluation for embolic source (carotid imaging, cardiac workup) and stroke prevention optimization despite anticoagulation
C) Refer only to optometry for refractive error
D) Start topical antibiotics for conjunctivitis
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Treat as amaurosis fugax/transient monocular vision loss—urgent evaluation for embolic source [carotid imaging, cardiac workup] and stroke prevention optimization despite anticoagulation).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Amaurosis fugax is transient monocular vision loss often described as a curtain descending over one eye, lasting minutes, representing retinal ischemia—analogous to TIA. Complete recovery does not eliminate stroke risk. Associated carotid bruit and atrial fibrillation increase embolic probability. Persistent vision loss suggests central retinal artery occlusion (stroke equivalent requiring emergent evaluation).

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Emboli (carotid atherosclerotic plaque, cardioembolic from AF) or hemodynamic hypoperfusion occlude central/branch retinal artery temporarily. Ipsilateral carotid stenosis ≥70% is a major source. Even on anticoagulation, patients may have additional atherosclerotic or hypoperfusion mechanisms.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Urgent same-day evaluation: detailed history (duration, pattern), BP, carotid auscultation, fundoscopy (Hollenhorst plaque), ECG for AF, consider echocardiography. Carotid duplex ultrasound assesses stenosis; CTA/MRA if indicated. Brain MRI may identify concurrent silent embolic strokes. Differentiate from migraine visual aura (positive scintillations, bilateral or homonymous field defects, headache), giant cell arteritis (headache, jaw claudication in older patients), and acute angle-closure glaucoma (painful red eye).

MANAGEMENT:
Stroke prevention pathway: optimize anticoagulation adherence for AF; manage vascular risk factors (BP, lipids, smoking). Symptomatic high-grade carotid stenosis may warrant endarterectomy/stenting per neurology/vascular surgery. Low-dose aspirin may be added in selected cases per specialist guidance when anticoagulation alone and embolic source persists. Educate on stroke warning signs and emergent care for prolonged vision loss.

KEY POINTS:
Transient monocular vision loss = retinal TIA—urgent embolic workup despite symptom resolution. Do not reassure without evaluation. AF plus carotid bruit raises embolic risk; anticoagulation does not obviate vascular imaging.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Urgent embolic workup and stroke prevention for amaurosis fugax).
- Sudden painless monocular vision loss with recovery suggests retinal TIA (amaurosis fugax).
- Requires carotid and cardiac evaluation despite full recovery and existing anticoagulation.
- Reassurance without workup, optometry alone, or antibiotics miss a stroke warning sign.

question: A 6-year-old boy has 2 days of otalgia, fever 38.5°C, and irritability. Otoscopy shows bulging, erythematous tympanic membrane with obscured landmarks and limited mobility on pneumatic otoscopy. He has no mastoid tenderness or neurologic signs. Which treatment is most appropriate?
options: A) Oral amoxicillin 80–90 mg/kg/day divided BID for 5–10 days (or amoxicillin-clavulanate if recent amoxicillin or high local failure rates)
B) Topical antibiotic drops only without examining tympanic membrane status
C) Oral azithromycin as universal first-line regardless of allergy history
D) Observation only in all children under 12 regardless of severity
explanation:
CORRECT: A (Oral amoxicillin 80–90 mg/kg/day divided BID for 5–10 days [or amoxicillin-clavulanate if recent amoxicillin or high local failure rates]).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Acute otitis media (AOM) presents with otalgia, fever, irritability in young children, and bulging erythematous TM with poor mobility on pneumatic otoscopy—distinguishing from otitis media with effusion (retracted TM, no acute inflammation). Severe illness (moderate-severe otalgia, fever ≥39°C), bilateral disease in children <2, or otorrhea with AOM warrant antibiotic therapy per AAP guidelines.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Eustachian tube dysfunction after viral URI allows bacterial ascent (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, Moraxella catarrhalis). Bulging TM reflects middle-ear pressure and inflammation. Complications include mastoiditis, perforation, and rarely intracranial extension if untreated in severe cases.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Diagnosis requires acute onset and middle-ear effusion (bulging TM, impaired mobility, air-fluid level, perforation with purulent otorrhea) plus inflammation. Distinguish external otitis (tragal tenderness, canal edema). Red TM alone from crying/fever insufficient. Assess for mastoid tenderness, meningismus, facial nerve palsy (urgent).

MANAGEMENT:
Antibiotics for children meeting severe criteria or age <2 with bilateral AOM; shared decision for unilateral nonsevere in older children. First-line amoxicillin 80–90 mg/kg/day (high-dose targets penicillin-resistant pneumococcus); amoxicillin-clavulanate if amoxicillin within 30 days or treatment failure. Duration 5–10 days per severity/age. Analgesia (acetaminophen/ibuprofen) essential. Topical drops alone treat otitis externa, not intact TM AOM. Follow-up if no improvement in 48–72 hours.

KEY POINTS:
Bulging TM with fever in child = AOM requiring antibiotics per severity/age guidelines—not observation for severe case or topical drops alone. High-dose amoxicillin first-line; azithromycin only for penicillin allergy.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: A (High-dose amoxicillin for acute otitis media with bulging TM and fever).
- Bulging erythematous immobile tympanic membrane with fever confirms AOM in a child.
- First-line: amoxicillin 80–90 mg/kg/day; amoxicillin-clavulanate if recent amoxicillin or high failure rates.
- Topical drops alone, universal azithromycin, or observation in severe young child are incorrect.

question: A 5-year-old unvaccinated child presents with high fever, drooling, muffled voice, and stridor at rest in tripod position. He appears toxic. Oxygen saturation is 92% on room air. Which immediate action is most appropriate?
options: A) Aggressive oropharyngeal examination with tongue depressor in clinic to confirm diagnosis

B) Do not agitate or examine throat; keep child calm, administer supplemental oxygen, call emergency services for airway management and hospital transfer—suspect epiglottitis or severe supraglottic obstruction
C) Prescribe oral amoxicillin and send home
D) Nebulized albuterol alone for presumed asthma
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Do not agitate or examine throat; keep child calm, administer supplemental oxygen, call emergency services for airway management and hospital transfer—suspect epiglottitis or severe supraglottic obstruction).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Acute epiglottitis (now rare post-Hib vaccine) presents with rapid onset high fever, severe sore throat, drooling (inability to swallow), muffled “hot potato” voice, stridor, and toxic appearance—classically leaning forward (tripod). Unlike croup, minimal cough and more abrupt deterioration. Bacterial supraglottitis can progress to complete airway obstruction within hours.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Haemophilus influenzae type b historically; now Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and other organisms in unvaccinated or immunocompromised. Supraglottic edema obstructs airway; manipulation may trigger laryngospasm and arrest.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Clinical diagnosis in unstable patient—do NOT perform tongue blade examination in office (may precipitate obstruction). Lateral neck X-ray may show thumbprint sign if child stable in controlled setting. Direct visualization reserved for operating room with anesthesia/ENT prepared for intubation. Distinguish from croup (barking cough, gradual URI prodrome, steeple sign) and retropharyngeal abscess (neck stiffness, limited extension).

MANAGEMENT:
Airway first: allow child to remain in preferred position, minimize procedures, supplemental oxygen, emergent EMS/hospital transfer to facility with pediatric airway capability. IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone) after airway secured. Corticosteroids and racemic epinephrine may be used per protocol in controlled setting. Intubation/tracheostomy if impending obstruction. Unvaccinated status warrants Hib consideration and public health notification.

Primary care must recognize and not attempt office throat exam.

KEY POINTS:
Toxic child with drooling, stridor, tripod posture: epiglottitis until proven otherwise—no throat exam in office; emergent airway management. Oral antibiotics outpatient or albuterol alone are dangerous.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Do not examine throat; oxygen, EMS, controlled airway management).
- Drooling, muffled voice, stridor at rest, and tripod posture suggest epiglottitis/supraglottic obstruction.
- Avoid agitating or examining oropharynx in clinic; emergent hospital transfer for airway control.
- Office throat exam, outpatient amoxicillin, or albuterol alone risk fatal obstruction.

question: A 32-year-old contact lens wearer presents with acute red eye, foreign body sensation, photophobia, and watery discharge. Visual acuity is slightly reduced. Slit-lamp exam shows corneal fluorescein uptake in a dendritic pattern. Which management is most appropriate?
options: A) Topical corticosteroid drops to reduce inflammation
B) Oral or topical antiviral therapy (e.g., oral valacyclovir) and ophthalmology referral; discontinue contact lenses; avoid steroids which can worsen HSV keratitis
C) Topical antibiotic drops only for bacterial conjunctivitis
D) Continue contact lens wear with lubricating drops
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Oral or topical antiviral therapy [e.g., oral valacyclovir] and ophthalmology referral; discontinue contact lenses; avoid steroids which can worsen HSV keratitis).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Herpes simplex keratitis presents with unilateral red eye, pain, photophobia, blurred vision, and dendritic corneal ulcer on fluorescein staining—branching epithelial defects with terminal bulbs pathognomonic for HSV. Contact lens wear increases risk of other corneal infections (Pseudomonas) but dendritic pattern indicates HSV. Reduced acuity signals corneal involvement requiring urgent ophthalmology care.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
HSV-1 reactivation in trigeminal ganglion infects corneal epithelium; epithelial disease may progress to stromal keratitis and scarring with vision loss. Topical corticosteroids on epithelial HSV can enlarge ulcer, prolong infection, and increase stromal involvement risk.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Fluorescein exam showing dendritic ulcer; consider HSV PCR/culture if atypical. Differentiate bacterial keratitis (contact lens, dense infiltrate, purulent discharge), acanthamoeba (severe pain out of proportion), and UV keratitis. Red flags: hypopyon, large infiltrate, scleritis—urgent ophthalmology.

MANAGEMENT:
Oral valacyclovir or topical antivirals (trifluridine, ganciclovir gel per local availability) for epithelial keratitis; ophthalmology co-management for follow-up and stromal disease. Avoid topical steroids unless ophthalmologist treats concomitant stromal inflammation with antiviral cover. Discontinue contact lenses until healed; educate on recurrence. Bacterial conjunctivitis therapy alone is ineffective and delays appropriate antiviral treatment.

KEY POINTS:
Dendritic corneal ulcer = HSV keratitis—antiviral therapy, ophthalmology referral, no steroids. Contact lens wear requires cessation during active keratitis. Topical antibiotics alone miss viral etiology.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Antiviral therapy, ophthalmology referral, stop contacts, avoid steroids).
- Dendritic fluorescein uptake on cornea indicates herpes simplex keratitis.
- Treat with oral/topical antivirals; corticosteroids can worsen HSV corneal disease.
- Antibiotic conjunctivitis treatment or continued lens wear is inappropriate.

question: A 48-year-old woman with average breast cancer risk asks about mammography screening. She has no BRCA mutation, prior chest radiation, or prior breast cancer. Which recommendation aligns with the 2024 USPSTF update?
options: A) Begin biennial screening mammography at age 50 only; screening before 50 is never recommended
B) Offer biennial screening mammography starting at age 40 for women at average risk, continuing through at least age 74, with individualized decisions for ages 75 and older
C) Annual MRI only without mammography for all average-risk women
D) Screening mammography once at age 65 then stop regardless of health status
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Offer biennial screening mammography starting at age 40 for women at average risk, continuing through at least age 74, with individualized decisions for ages 75 and older).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Breast cancer often presents asymptomatically on screening; when symptomatic may cause palpable mass, skin dimpling, nipple discharge, or axillary adenopathy. Average-risk women benefit from early detection when treatment is less morbid and mortality lower. Younger-onset cancers occur and motivated USPSTF to lower starting age.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Most breast cancers are invasive ductal or lobular carcinomas arising from epithelial proliferation influenced by hormonal, genetic, and environmental factors. Screening detects preclinical disease before palpability.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
USPSTF 2024 (Grade B): biennial screening mammography for women 40–74 at average risk. Ages 75+: individualized based on health, life expectancy, prior screening history. High-risk women (BRCA, strong family history, chest radiation age 10–30, prior atypical hyperplasia) need earlier MRI/mammography per ACS/NCCN—not this protocol. Clinical breast exam alone is insufficient screening.

MANAGEMENT:
Shared decision-making discusses benefits (mortality reduction, especially 40–49 with smaller absolute benefit), harms (false positives, callbacks, overdiagnosis, anxiety), and breast density notification laws in many states (may warrant supplemental imaging per guidelines). Ensure follow-up for BI-RADS 0/4/5. Continue screening unless limited life expectancy (<10 years) or patient preference after informed discussion.

Primary care orders mammography, tracks results, and coordinates diagnostic workup for abnormalities.

KEY POINTS:
USPSTF 2024: biennial mammography from age 40–74 for average-risk women—not wait until 50 only. MRI-only or single mammogram at 65 is not standard. High-risk patients need enhanced protocols.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Biennial mammography starting at age 40 through at least 74).
- 2024 USPSTF recommends biennial screening mammography for average-risk women beginning at age 40.
- Continue through at least age 74; individualize for women 75 and older.
- Age 50-only start, MRI-only, or one-time screening at 65 do not match current guidance.

question: A 35-year-old man born in a country with high hepatitis B prevalence has no prior HBV testing. He is asymptomatic. Which screening approach is most appropriate?
options: A) No screening unless liver enzymes are elevated
B) One-time hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg), anti-HBs, and total anti-HBc testing per USPSTF to identify chronic infection, immunity, or susceptibility
C) Hepatitis B screening only for persons over age 65
D) Screen only if patient requests hepatitis C testing
explanation:
CORRECT: B (One-time hepatitis B surface antigen [HBsAg], anti-HBs, and total anti-HBc testing per USPSTF to identify chronic infection, immunity, or susceptibility).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Chronic hepatitis B (CHB) is often asymptomatic for decades yet causes cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma. Patients born in endemic regions (Asia, Africa, Pacific Islands, Eastern Europe) have higher prevalence. Normal ALT does not exclude active disease or need for treatment.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
HBV replicates in hepatocytes; chronic infection defined by HBsAg ≥6 months. Immune tolerance, immune active, inactive carrier, and resolved phases determine monitoring and antiviral need. Integration into host genome contributes to carcinogenesis even on treatment.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
USPSTF 2020 recommends screening adolescents and adults at increased risk including those born in countries with ≥2% HBsAg prevalence, HIV-positive persons, injection drug users, household contacts of HBV-infected persons, and men who have sex with men. Triple panel: HBsAg (current infection), anti-HBs (immunity from vaccine or recovery), anti-HBc (past exposure). Interpretation distinguishes susceptible (all negative), vaccinated (anti-HBs alone positive), resolved (anti-HBc + anti-HBs), chronic (HBsAg positive).

MANAGEMENT:
Chronic HBV: hepatology referral, HBV DNA, ALT, fibrosis assessment, HCC surveillance with ultrasound ± AFP in indicated patients, antiviral therapy (tenofovir/entecavir) when criteria met. Vaccinate susceptible household contacts; counsel on alcohol avoidance. Screen does not depend on elevated LFTs or age >65.

Primary care implements one-time risk-based screening and linkage to care.

KEY POINTS:
HBV screening: one-time HBsAg, anti-HBs, anti-HBc for high-prevalence birth country—not wait for abnormal LFTs. Distinct from HCV screening. Age 65 cutoff is incorrect.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (One-time HBsAg, anti-HBs, anti-HBc for high-risk patient).
- USPSTF recommends HBV screening for people born in high-prevalence countries regardless of symptoms.
- Triple serology identifies chronic infection, immunity, or need for vaccination.
- Normal liver enzymes or age limits do not exclude indicated screening.

question: A 58-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and hyperlipidemia asks about dietary pattern to reduce cardiovascular risk beyond medications. She is willing to change eating habits. Which recommendation has the strongest evidence in primary prevention?
options: A) Very low-carbohydrate ketogenic diet with unlimited saturated fat
B) Mediterranean-style dietary pattern emphasizing vegetables, fruits, whole grains, legumes, nuts, olive oil, and fish; limiting processed meats and refined carbohydrates
C) Strict juice-only cleanse for 30 days
D) Eliminate all dietary fat including nuts and olive oil
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Mediterranean-style dietary pattern emphasizing vegetables, fruits, whole grains, legumes, nuts, olive oil, and fish; limiting processed meats and refined carbohydrates).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Patients with diabetes and dyslipidemia carry elevated ASCVD risk requiring lifestyle plus pharmacotherapy. Dietary counseling in primary care significantly affects weight, glycemic control, blood pressure, and lipid profiles when sustained patterns are adopted—not short-term fad diets.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Mediterranean diet components reduce inflammation, improve insulin sensitivity, and favorably alter lipoproteins (lower LDL, higher HDL function) through monounsaturated fats, polyphenols, fiber, and omega-3 fatty acids. Processed meats and refined carbohydrates increase cardiometabolic risk via sodium, advanced glycation products, and glycemic load.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Assess current diet, readiness to change, food security, and cultural preferences. Review medications (statins, GLP-1, SGLT2i) that complement diet. BMI, BP, A1c, and lipids track response. Refer registered dietitian when complex or poorly controlled.

MANAGEMENT:
PREDIMED and numerous cohort studies support Mediterranean diet for primary and secondary cardiovascular prevention and diabetes management. Practical counseling: half plate non-starchy vegetables, whole grains, fatty fish twice weekly, olive oil as primary fat, limit red/processed meat and sugar-sweetened beverages. Very low-carb/ketogenic diets may improve short-term glycemia but long-term cardiovascular effects depend on fat quality—unlimited saturated fat is not recommended. Juice cleanses and zero-fat diets are unsupported and potentially harmful.

Coordinate with diabetes self-management education.

KEY POINTS:
Mediterranean dietary pattern has strongest evidence for cardiometabolic risk reduction in primary care—not juice cleanses or unlimited saturated fat keto. Emphasize sustainable pattern over restrictive fads.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Mediterranean-style diet with vegetables, whole grains, olive oil, fish).
- Mediterranean dietary pattern has robust evidence for cardiovascular and diabetes risk reduction.
- Limit processed meats and refined carbohydrates; include nuts and healthy fats.
- Juice cleanses, zero-fat diets, or unlimited saturated fat keto are not evidence-based primary prevention.

question: A 47-year-old man drinks 5 beers nightly and scores 18 on the AUDIT questionnaire ( hazardous drinking). He wants to cut down but is not alcohol-dependent with withdrawal history. Which evidence-based primary care intervention is most appropriate?
options: A) Brief intervention using motivational interviewing and structured brief counseling; consider naltrexone or acamprosate if goal is reduction/abstinence with ongoing support
B) Immediate inpatient detoxification for all AUDIT scores above 10
C) No discussion of alcohol because patient has not requested referral
D) Prescribe benzodiazepines daily for long-term anxiety related to drinking
explanation:
CORRECT: A (Brief intervention using motivational interviewing and structured brief counseling; consider naltrexone or acamprosate if goal is reduction/abstinence with ongoing support).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Hazardous drinking (AUDIT ≥8 men, ≥7 women) increases risk of liver disease, hypertension, cancer, injury, and depression yet often unrecognized in primary care. This patient acknowledges desire to cut down—ideal for brief intervention. Absence of withdrawal history makes outpatient management appropriate.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Alcohol use disorder exists on a spectrum from hazardous use to dependence with withdrawal. Chronic intake affects GABA/glutamate balance; dependence with withdrawal requires medically supervised detox—not this case.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
AUDIT and AUDIT-C screen quantity and consequences. Assess DSM-5 criteria for severity, CAGE/AUDIT for tracking. Labs: AST/ALT, GGT, CBC (MCV), CMP. Evaluate comorbid depression/anxiety, sleep disorders, and polysubstance use. CIWA-Ar if withdrawal suspected.

MANAGEMENT:
USPSTF recommends screening and brief behavioral counseling in adults. SBIRT model: Screen, Brief intervention, Referral to treatment. Motivational interviewing explores ambivalence and sets SMART goals. Pharmacotherapy: naltrexone (reduces craving, relapse) or acamprosate (abstinence support) for moderate-severe AUD without contraindications; disulfiram if highly motivated. Inpatient detox reserved for severe dependence, withdrawal risk, or failed outpatient trials—not AUDIT 18 alone without dependence. Long-term benzodiazepines worsen alcohol outcomes.

Follow-up visits monitor consumption, liver health, and mental health.

KEY POINTS:
Hazardous drinking: brief intervention plus consider naltrexone/acamprosate—not mandatory inpatient detox or ignoring AUDIT. Daily benzodiazepines are harmful long-term strategy.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: A (Brief motivational intervention; consider naltrexone or acamprosate).
- AUDIT score 18 indicates hazardous drinking warranting SBIRT-style counseling in primary care.
- Outpatient brief intervention and pharmacotherapy appropriate without withdrawal history.
- Inpatient detox for all scores, avoiding discussion, or chronic benzodiazepines are incorrect.

question: A 44-year-old man with BMI 32, fasting glucose 110 mg/dL, and HbA1c 6.0% has no diabetes diagnosis. He is sedentary and asks how to prevent progression to type 2 diabetes. Which recommendation has the strongest evidence?
options: A) Metformin 1000 mg daily alone without lifestyle change as first-line for all prediabetes

B) Intensive lifestyle modification program targeting ≥7% weight loss and ≥150 minutes/week moderate physical activity (Diabetes Prevention Program model), with metformin considered if lifestyle not achieved or high-risk features
C) No intervention until HbA1c reaches 6.5%
D) Strict fasting diet under 800 kcal/day indefinitely without medical supervision
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Intensive lifestyle modification program targeting ≥7% weight loss and ≥150 minutes/week moderate physical activity [Diabetes Prevention Program model], with metformin considered if lifestyle not achieved or high-risk features).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Prediabetes (IFG 100–125, HbA1c 5.7–6.4%, or OGTT 140–199) identifies patients at high short-term risk of type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease. Many are asymptomatic; primary care detection enables prevention before complications.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Insulin resistance and beta-cell dysfunction progress over years; weight gain and inactivity accelerate decline. Lifestyle changes improve insulin sensitivity and preserve beta-cell function more effectively than observation.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Confirm prediabetes with repeat testing. Assess cardiovascular risk, fatty liver, OSA, and family history. Identify patients who may benefit most from pharmacotherapy (BMI ≥35, age <60, history of gestational diabetes, rising A1c despite lifestyle).

MANAGEMENT:
Diabetes Prevention Program (DPP) demonstrated 58% relative risk reduction with lifestyle intervention (7% weight loss, 150 min/week moderate activity) versus 31% with metformin alone. ADA recommends structured DPP or equivalent for all prediabetes. Metformin adjunct for high-risk when lifestyle insufficient—not monotherapy replacing lifestyle. Waiting until diabetes threshold misses prevention window. Very-low-calorie diets require medical supervision and are not first-line.

Refer to CDC-recognized DPP programs when available; track weight, activity, and A1c annually.

KEY POINTS:
Prediabetes: DPP-style lifestyle (7% weight loss, 150 min/week activity) is first-line—stronger evidence than metformin alone. Do not defer until diabetes diagnostic criteria met.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (DPP lifestyle program first; metformin if needed for high-risk).
- Prediabetes (A1c 6.0%, IFG 110) warrants intensive lifestyle intervention per DPP evidence.
- Target ≥7% weight loss and ≥150 minutes/week moderate activity.
- Metformin alone without lifestyle, waiting for diabetes, or unsupervised extreme fasting are incorrect.
